input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A 1/10-scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology, featuring main hardware layers consisting of lower and upper level chassis components and autonomy elements. The operational domain is Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC), characterized by resource-constrained electronic control units (ECUs) requiring offloading of critical computations, real-time data monitoring, and remote management capabilities to address safety-critical system demands.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three core services: (1) Data Monitoring, which includes real-time visualization of vehicle state data, projected driving paths, and detected object tracks via a user interface (UI), alongside data saving and report generation capabilities; (2) Computation Offloading, where the ACC system's lead vehicle detection, speed calculation based on distance to the lead vehicle, and actuation commands are offloaded from the vehicle's ECUs to cloud-based resources; (3) Remote Management, enabling operators to remotely enable/disable ACC, set desired speeds, and send emergency braking commands through the UI. These services support operators for system monitoring and management and developers for troubleshooting using collected data.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation utilizes the ISO 23247 reference architecture for standardization, Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) middleware for inter-component communication, NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 SDK for GPU-accelerated processing, Python 3.8 and C++ for software development, MATLAB/Simulink for simulation and modeling, and a SQL database for structured data storage and reporting capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system requires 100 ms clock synchronization accuracy for computation offloading in safety-critical vehicular applications, as mandated by industry standards, with hardware limitations necessitating this specific timing constraint.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin architecture consists of three hierarchical entities—Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity—each deployed on separate hardware components. The User Entity includes a split User Interface FE into 3D Visualization and Control Panel subcomponents, while the Physical Twin Entity hosts general automotive functionality like Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking, deviating from ISO 23247's OME structure.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system follows ISO 23247 RA architecture, mapping software components to Functional Entities (FEs) implemented as ROS2 packages. Each entity (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User) is deployed on distinct hardware, with coordination mechanisms including 100 ms clock synchronization for vehicular safety. The Physical Twin Entity extends beyond ISO 23247's OME by hosting resource-specific automotive functions.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin employs DBSCAN clustering for raw LiDAR point clouds and Kalman filtering for object trajectory tracking, with all data stored in a SQL database managing object tracks and ego vehicle state.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation converts desired speed into motor voltage commands; lower level chassis control manages steering and speed of the vehicle.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors include a Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR and Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera, with data processed by an NVIDIA Jetson NX platform for real-time autonomy operations.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Raw LiDAR point cloud data is processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software component to transform 2D plane points into structured data. Vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving paths are transmitted via ROS2 for internal component communication and UDP over Wi-Fi for inter-entity communication. The Data Translation component handles serialization, message type alignment, and unit conversions for all data streams.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands for Motor Actuation and Steering Servo Actuation are generated by the ACC Controlling FE and Control Panel User Interface FE. The Data Translation component processes these commands through serialization, message type alignment, and unit conversions. Communication between software components uses ROS2, and inter-entity communication uses UDP over Wi-Fi.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The network infrastructure uses ROS2 for internal software component communication and UDP over Wi-Fi for inter-entity transmission. The system enforces a maximum network latency of 100 ms, with each entity deployed on separate hardware components forming a distributed topology.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Each Functional Entity (FE) is implemented as an individual ROS2 package and deployed on NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano hardware platforms, with each entity hosted on distinct hardware components to ensure dedicated resource allocation.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process involves defining system requirements (Section IV.A), designing the architecture using the ISO 23247-2 reference architecture with Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity (Section IV.B), and implementing Functional Entities such as OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) for data collection and device control (Section IV.C).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The system provides user interaction through a User Entity interface for data monitoring (viewing and saving reports) and remote management of the Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) subsystem, including enabling/disabling features and setting speed parameters via the User Interface Functional Entity (Section III.A and IV.C).
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with ISO 23247-2:2021 Reference Architecture, which defines entity-based structure including Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity; OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) is referenced as an implementation example for Data Collection and Device Control Entity to ensure interoperability.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction provides a highly specific and accurate summary of the physical twin, correctly identifying it as a '1/10-scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle' that was 'retrofitted'. It accurately details the hardware architecture (lower and upper chassis) and the operational domain (ACC). It also correctly links the resource constraints of the ECUs to the need for computation offloading, monitoring, and safety-critical management, all of which are explicitly stated in the text.

The description accurately enumerates and details the three specific services mentioned in the text: Data Monitoring (real-time visualization, reports, data saving), Computation Offloading (moving ACC logic like lead vehicle detection and speed calculation to the DT/cloud), and Remote Management (enabling/disabling ACC, setting speed, emergency braking). It also correctly identifies the target users (operators and developers) and their specific needs (monitoring/management vs troubleshooting).

The extraction lists all specified tools, frameworks, and languages found in the document, including the reference architecture, middleware, SDK, programming languages, simulation tools, and database type, assigning them the correct roles.

Supporting quotes:
- "investigate the fitness of the ISO 23247 reference architecture"
- "Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware"
- "Data Storage Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database"

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the 100 ms synchronization accuracy and latency requirements, and correctly links them to computation offloading and hardware limitations. However, it slightly misattributes the specific 'mandated by industry standards' clause to the clock synchronization, whereas the text states that standards mandate stricter accuracy than the 100 ms required by the hardware (3GPP is cited for latency, not sync). Despite this minor conflation, it is specific and technically grounded. Supported by quotes: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms', 'We require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations', 'The DTS shall maintain network latency of at most 100 ms from the vehicle to the DT Entity'.

The extraction accurately describes the three entities (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User), their deployment on separate hardware, the specific splitting of the User Interface FE into 3D Visualization and Control Panel, and the deviation from ISO 23247 regarding the Physical Twin Entity hosting general automotive functions like Point Cloud Clustering. It is highly detailed and fully supported. Supported by quotes: 'Considering that each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component', 'the singular User Interface FE is instantiated in the 3D Visualization and Control Panel components', 'Physical Twin Entity... marks a departure from the ISO 23247 RA'.

This description comprehensively covers the architecture, mapping of software to Functional Entities (FEs) via ROS2 packages, distinct hardware deployment, and coordination mechanisms like clock synchronization. It also correctly notes the extension of the Physical Twin Entity beyond the standard OME. It is in-depth and well-supported. Supported by quotes: 'We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design phase', 'implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package', 'each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text focuses on the internal architecture of the Digital Twin system (Physical, Digital, User entities) and the specific vehicle case study. While 'cloud-based fleet coordination' is mentioned in the abstract as a general industry need, and 'Data Translation' handles internal data formats, there is no description of specific integration with external systems, enterprise systems, or interoperability standards with other Digital Twins. Therefore, 'Not in Document' is correct.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. Raw point cloud data from the LiDAR sensor must be processed by the _Point Cloud_ _Clustering & Tracking_ SW component, which turns the raw point cloud, represented as individual points on a 2D plane,

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the data flow from physical sensors (LiDAR) to software components (Clustering & Tracking) and the role of the Data Translation component. It correctly identifies ROS2 and Wi-Fi as communication media. However, it claims 'UDP' which is not fully supported by the text (which ends with 'using U'), and it describes LiDAR data as '2D plane points' whereas the text refers to 'raw LiDAR point clouds'. Supported by quotes: 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking Clusters raw LiDAR point clouds', 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network', 'This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions'.

The extraction correctly identifies the sources of control commands (ACC Controlling FE, Control Panel), the targets (Motor Actuation, Steering Servo), and the processing done by the Data Translation component. It accurately notes the use of ROS2 for internal communication. However, it incorrectly specifies 'UDP' for inter-entity communication as the source text is truncated at 'using U'. Supported by quotes: 'Control Panel Interface for the user to send remote management commands', 'ACC Determines lead vehicle and computes following speed', 'This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions'.

The extraction accurately details the network infrastructure, specifying ROS2 for internal communication and Wi-Fi for inter-entity transmission. It correctly includes the 100 ms latency requirement and the distributed topology. The mention of 'UDP' is technically unsupported by the provided snippet due to truncation, but the rest is well-grounded. Supported by quotes: 'R7. The DTS shall maintain network latency of at most 100 ms', 'each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component', 'inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network'.

While the extraction correctly states that each FE is an individual ROS2 package and deployed on separate hardware, it incorrectly claims that all entities are deployed on 'NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano hardware platforms'. The source text confirms the Jetson Orin Nano for the physical twin's autonomy components but explicitly describes the Digital Twin Entity as 'external' and offloaded, without specifying the Jetson Orin Nano for that entity. Thus, the specific hosting platform claim for the whole system is unsupported. Supported by quotes: 'implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package', 'autonomy components include... NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano microcomputer', 'offloading only the ACC system from the vehicle to the DT'.

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_5_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document'. While the text mentions DTs being used as a 'product lifecycle management tool' and describes the steps of the specific case study (requirements, architecture, implementation), it does not define the specific 'lifecycle phases of the Digital Twin' such as representation types at different phases, transition management between phases, or lifecycle governance approaches as requested by the characteristic definition. Therefore, the statement that it is not in the document is correct.

The extraction accurately summarizes the engineering process steps described in Section IV of the document, including requirements definition, architectural design using ISO 23247-2, and implementation details involving Functional Entities and OPC UA. Evidence includes: 'starting from the definition of DTS requirements (Sec. IV-A)', 'We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design phase', and references to Functional Entities. However, the extraction does not cover 'quality assurance practices, testing and validation approaches, version control, and evolution management strategies' mentioned in the characteristic definition, as these are not detailed in the source text.

The description correctly details the decision support and user interaction capabilities of the system, specifically the User Entity interface for data monitoring (viewing, saving reports) and remote management of the ACC (enabling/disabling, setting speed). This is well-supported by the text: 'The DT presented in this case will contain a user interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports' and 'The UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed'. The extraction is specific and accurate.

The extraction is highly specific and detailed, accurately reflecting the source text. It correctly identifies 'ISO 23247-2:2021' as the reference architecture and lists the specific entities: 'Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)', 'Data Collection and Device Control Entity', 'Digital Twin Entity', and 'User Entity'. It also correctly references 'OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)' as an example for implementation. Quotes: 'Part 2 of the standard provides a detailed, layered RA... The Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)... Data Collection and Device Control Entity... Digital Twin Entity... User Entity' and 'one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture (UA) of IEC 62541 to facilitate the implementation'.

block_6_judge.reasoning: The source documents focus on the engineering architecture (ISO 23247), functional requirements (e.g., R3: 'The DTS shall store the ego vehicle state data on a device external to the vehicle'), and implementation details. They do not contain information regarding data governance, ownership policies, privacy regulations, or consent management.

While the documents mention the ACC system is 'safety-critical' and that the DTS must respect this 'nature of the system' through low latency (R7), they do not describe specific security measures such as cybersecurity protocols, access control, or authentication. They also do not detail fail-safe mechanisms or threat mitigation strategies beyond the general adherence to ISO 15622 for control strategy.
